author: @jarlingesandvik9883
text: REAL reinventing the wheel her is my idee. Put chambers in the circle and and put a hydraulic senterhub motor. Let the load of the bike runs the hubm. From the pressure made in the excenter Chambers. Like a punkture tire. The pressure in the camber pulls the hub motor to the next camber. The fluid inside is moving from hige pressure camber to hub and back to low pressure camber . Motor and back to low pressure camber. And the hydraulic hub. Motor is moving the wheel to the next faze of camber compression.

author: @wongowonga
text: I can see you adapting this for a wind turbine. With one revolution of the wheel you would be able to drive many internal cogs at the same time (with decent ratio) which in turn could be used to generate. I can see torque being a potential problem because of the friction but maybe the idea could be developed further... just thinking out loud incase it inspires... Love your ideas, it seriously wouldn't surprise me if you discover something ground-breaking somewhere along the line.

author: @ChristieNel
text: Proper job!  Would it be better if you had one "gear" straight down to carry all the load?  With two loaded gears I'd guess there is a deforming force on the rim.  Also, there will be a sheer force on the "wishbone".

author: @joohop
text:
Brilliant Work Fella
Bless Up :)

author: @ScarletFlames1
text: Hmm, this design has potential for electric motorbikes. it frees up a large amount of space inside the hub of the wheels to house the motor without placing the stress of the bike itself on the motor, because using a brushless motor also allows for a flat design to fit an extremely powerful motor inside the hub while protecting it from wear and tear by having it basically be free-floating rather than bearing the weight of the entire motorbike. Then you can have a rather ridiculous amount of batteries housed inside the rest of the thing as it would otherwise be an empty shell..

author: @timothygorman7528

DOC!  DOC!  (Emmet Brown - Back to the future) -

Luke is a lucky guy - as he gets to hang out with you all day :)   

You two are just soooo awesome. Thank you!

author: @richardr1891
text: Wonderful design and effort ... I was thinking about the structure of the Spoke Wheel and how the Spokes are placed a few inches apart in order to take stress at a consistent rate when the wheel is turning .. is having three points of contact not the same as having three spokes ... . I am confident your bike will function but do you think the Rim without the spokes will be able to take the stress and stay in round not warp or fracture  ... not trying to be a critic for the sake being critical but was wondering what your thoughts were ... you must have a lot of confidence in the quality of the wheel rim and the metal it's made out of ...

author: @Corum001
text: Does the spoke removal effect the wheel strength and does it run true?

replies.author: @nikolaasgerard2454
replies.text: I'm afraid spokes are an essential part of this kind of wheel. Even if there's only 1 spoke missing or with the wrong tension it has an effect on the wheel's strength.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Excellent Rob, that system could also be used by removing the tyre and attaching large blades so it turns 3 generators which can be attached in parallel to increase the current or in series to increase the voltage. The inside of the rim could also house magnets which pass a number of coils lined up between the cogs on the wishbone.

author: @DaymeEdna
text: What a wonderful build! Thankfully you stopped short of following Mr Garrison’s “IT Bike” design on South Park.

author: @scotttovey
text:
OK cool.
But how does that translate into the gear ratio of the original hub?
If it's not the same, it may end up being terribly hard to peddle.
The other question is; can this be used to create a multi geared bike?
If the object is an e-bike, multi-geared bike is  not necessary, unless you want it to be able to do sixty down the express lane and get yourself killed.

author: @shedactivist
text: This design is crying out for an electric motor drive on one or more of the pinion wheels.

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: No rear breaks!!!   I thought you were going to change it to a belt drive!!

replies.author: @another3997
replies.text: Is his rear so tired it needs a break? Still, whilst he's having a break, he can think about adding a rear brake. 😉

replies.author: @BoB4jjjjs
replies.text:
@another3997 I doubt the wheel would have stayed in place anyway!
If he had made it a belt drive it would have been smoother and he would have still had breaks.  I don't know if his rear is tired or sore, but he sure didn't want to sit on the bike and crank it :-D

author: @Moist_yet_Crispy

author: @robertpitt8418
text: Wonderful engineering - how on earth did you ever survive all those years without welding  : )  well done rob, and thanks for sharing your work .

author: @ProlificInvention
text: Genius man.  I like it, I also like the Golden Eagle bicycle engine/motor kit that utilizes a large diameter pulley almost the size of the wheel itself.  I am mounting an AGNI 95 motor which is a unique design of 15kw capable pancake D/C motor, that is also very efficient.  If you look up AGNI/Lynch motors I'm almost sure you would get a kick out of the interesting story of Cedric Lynch's axial gapped brushed DC motor.

author: @Mr.Unacceptable
text: Is there more or less friction with this wheel rather than the chain? Did you add weight or remove weight for this modification?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I may be off, but my initial impression is that it's going to have significantly more friction compared to a metal chain + metal gears, partly because there is more surface area involved and because a metal chain + gears has less friction than belt style drive. 

    But points for thinking outside the box.

author: @angelusmendez5084

I'm glad it didn't turn out to be a hopeless wheel 😂

Amazing project, thanks!

author: @goat-shaw
text: think that would be ideal to turn into ebike

author: @FFND16N
text:
Since a bicycle rim is designed to work in concert with tensioned spokes about a hub, wouldn't such an arrangement suffer deformation and eventual collapse under the 'normal' riding weight & road impacts of a human?

Otherwise, absolutely fantastic idea; I  immediately think of this being used as both a large, roller bearing ring/race and spur drive contact point for a very large, DIY VAWT axis. Many people attempt to use small, generic bearings, repurposed from bicycles, washing machines & cars on homebuilt wind turbines, but I always thought a 'larger diameter' bearing track was supposed to be a more durable bearing surface for high speed rotations?
